ensemble.other_hypothesis: SLE/vasculitis-related mesenteric ischemia or bowel inflammation, small bowel obstruction, cholecystostomy-related complication (tube dislodgement, biloma, abscess), recurrent line-related or intra-abdominal sepsis, bacterial overgrowth with ileus/obstruction
ensemble.information_gap: The prior CT in the ED was a preliminary read showing 'normal pancreas' but the full picture of the abdomen — including the cholecystostomy tube position, gallbladder status, bowel, mesentery, and any new fluid collections or abscess — has not been formally characterized in the context of this acute worsening, elevated lipase/amylase, neutrophilia, low-grade fever, and recent Serratia sepsis
ensemble.expected_finding: The physician is hoping to identify or exclude: (1) acute pancreatitis or peripancreatic inflammation not captured on prelim read, (2) cholecystostomy tube complication (dislodgement, pericholecystic abscess, biloma), (3) mesenteric ischemia or vasculitis-related bowel wall thickening given SLE history, (4) new intra-abdominal fluid collection or abscess as source of fever and neutrophilia, (5) bowel obstruction given one-week constipation
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: Given the complex multi-system background (SLE, cholecystostomy, TPN-dependent, recent Serratia sepsis, elevated pancreatic enzymes, neutrophilia, low-grade fever, and worsening abdominal pain), a formal contrast-enhanced CT abdomen is appropriate to comprehensively characterize the abdominal pathology that the preliminary ED read and chest X-ray cannot fully exclude.
grounding: History: 'GB was noted to be edematous with gall stones... Cholecystostomy was placed and pt was told she should have her gall bladder removed'
History: 'She was admitted to ___ from ___ to ___ with Serratia sepsis. Source was R PICC line'
History: 'last BM was at ___ (over 1 week ago). +passing gas'
History: 'Normal pancreas on CT abd/pelvis prelim read'
Laboratory: 'Amylase: 136.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-100.0)'
Laboratory: 'Lipase: 154.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)'
Laboratory: 'Neutrophils: 91.8 % (ref 50.0-70.0)'
History: 'T 99.8 in the office'
History: 'most likely secondary to a bacterial overgrowth problem or vasculitis involving the small bowel'
Physical: '+cholecystostomy with biliary drainage'
ensemble.reasoning: This patient has worsening abdominal pain, low-grade fever, neutrophilia (91.8%), elevated lipase (154) and amylase (136), a cholecystostomy tube in place, and a recent history of Serratia sepsis — all in the context of SLE with known mesenteric vasculitis risk. The preliminary ED CT read only commented on the pancreas being normal, leaving the gallbladder/cholecystostomy status, bowel, mesentery, and any occult fluid collections uncharacterized. A formal contrast-enhanced CT abdomen is ordered to comprehensively evaluate for cholecystostomy complications, mesenteric ischemia, bowel obstruction, or new abscess that could explain the acute decompensation.

vindication.actual_finding: CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast showed a normally appearing pancreas, cholecystostomy tube in place without perigallbladder collection, no intra-abdominal fluid collections or abscess, no bowel wall thickening, no obstruction, and unremarkable bowel throughout.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The scan failed to identify any of the five anticipated pathologies — no pancreatitis, no cholecystostomy tube complication, no mesenteric/bowel abnormality, no new fluid collection or abscess, and no obstruction — leaving the source of the patient's acute worsening unexplained by intra-abdominal imaging.
